title: Patterned fronts in the wake of a parameter ramp in the complex Ginzburg Landau equation
authors: Ryan Goh, Benjamin Krewson, Nilay Patel, Kiersten Ratcliff
abstract: We study of the formation of pattern-forming fronts in the presence of a rigidly-propagating parameter ramp which is slowly-varying in space. In the context of the prototypical supercritical complex Ginzburg-Landau equation, we show that not only the leading order front interface, but also the selected spatial wave number is governed by the transition of the ramp between absolute and convective instability. The slow ramp then induces a further delay of the front interface and perturbation of the selected wave number, controlled by the slow passage near a complex fold of strong- and weak-stable eigenspaces. To analyze the behavior near this fold, we perform a multiple scales analysis to predict the higher-order front interface delay in terms of zeros and poles of a complex Airy quotient inner solution. We confirm these predictions with numerical continuation of heteroclinics in the associated traveling wave equation. We also numerically characterize their spectral stability, finding accumulation of eigenvalues consistent with previous results on slow absolute spectrum. We then show the leading-order absolute/convective instability heuristic accurately describes selected wave numbers in an analogous slowly-ramped Swift-Hohenberg equation.
summary: This paper analyzes how a moving, slowly-varying parameter ramp dictates the properties of pattern-forming fronts in the complex Ginzburg-Landau equation. It shows that the transition from convective to absolute instability governs the leading-order dynamics, while higher-order corrections follow a specific scaling law derived from an Airy-type inner solution. The findings are validated through both analytical asymptotic expansions and numerical simulations.
main_problem: Investigating how a slowly-varying, propagating parameter ramp influences the formation, location, and wavenumber selection of pattern-forming fronts.
main_result: The transition between convective and absolute instability (AC instability) determines the leading-order front location and temporal frequency, with higher-order delays scaling as epsilon^(2/3) via an Airy quotient inner solution.
method: Geometric Singular Perturbation Theory (GSPT), multiple scales analysis, and numerical continuation of heteroclinics and spectral stability.
model_or_system: The supercritical complex Ginzburg-Landau equation (CGLE) with a spatially and temporally varying parameter ramp, and the Swift-Hohenberg equation for validation.
key_observables: Front interface location (delay), selected spatial wavenumber, temporal oscillation frequency, and spectral stability (eigenvalue accumulation).
important_parameters: Ramp speed (c), ramp slope (epsilon), dispersion/nonlinear coefficients (alpha, gamma), and the transition between convective and absolute instability.
main_assumptions: The ramp is slowly-varying (epsilon << 1); the system is in the Benjamin-Feir stable regime (1 + alpha*gamma > 0).
figures_summary: Numerical simulations of pattern formation; plots of the complex Airy quotient function (zeros, poles, and contours); and comparisons of numerical wavenumber selection and front position against analytical AC instability predictions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ramped CGLE, develops a traveling wave reduction, applies GSPT and multiple scales analysis to derive an Airy-based inner solution, performs numerical continuation to validate the manifold structure, and concludes with spectral stability analysis and validation using the Swift-Hohenberg equation.
why_interesting: The study of pattern formation driven by parameter ramps and the transition between convective and absolute instabilities is highly relevant to non-equilibrium dynamics and the stability of driven-dissipative systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 3.1: Phase portraits of (3.4) with ε = 0, for a range of ˜µ values ˜µ < 0 (left), ˜µ > 0 (center left) both for ˜ω < 0, and ˜µ = 0, ˜ω = 0 (center right); Right: Critical equilibrium curves ˜z±,0(˜µ) for ˜µ ∼0 with α = −0.1, and ˜ω < 0 (small dashed) ˜ω = 0 (solid), and ˜ω > 0 (large dashed). Purple curves denote ˜z+,0(˜µ), orange denotes ˜z−,0(˜µ).

Figure 3.2: Comparison in the complex ˜z-plane of numerical slow manifold curves ˜z−,ε(˜µ) (solid colors), critical equilibrium curve ˜z−,0(˜µ) (dashed color), and approximate inner solution ˜zin −,ε(˜µ) (dashed black) for ˜µ ∼0 with values (ε, ˜ω) ≈(9.8, 7.3) × 10−3 (red), (4.5, −3.8) × 10−3 (blue), and (1.5, −1.5) × 10−3 (green); α = −0.1, c = 1. The ˜ω-values are O(ε2/3) and were determined by numerical continuation of the full problem, see Sec. 4 below.

Figure 3.3: Left: Plot in the complex s-plane of the function ˆz(s) for α = −0.1, lightness corresponds to |ˆz| while color denotes Arg ˆz, see scale on side; Right: Plot of the line Lθ (black) along with zeros sz,j = ˜σj/θ (open dots) and poles sp,j = σj/θ (closed dots) of ˆz and contours Rj for j = 1, 2, 3 (blue).

Figure 3.4: Singular unstable manifold W u 0 (rp, ˜zp, 1−µc) (green surface) in the ˜z, r space with the critical set Sp,0 (dark green line), for ˜µ ∈(0, 0.08) solved up to the section ˜Σδ = {˜κ = δ} (red). The fast fibers W uu 0 (rp, ˜zp, 1−µc) (green curves) with larger ˜q values correspond to larger ˜µ values; Also plotted are the critical sets S−,0 (yellow) and S+,0 (black) for a range of ˜µ values near 0; Plots give several values of ˜ω = {−1, 0, 1} × 10−3 (left to right, top to bottom); all with c = 1, α = −0.1, γ = −0.3.
